title: Sunday, July 22
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: July 22, 2018 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Eighth Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time (Year B, Revised Common Lectionary). The likely readings were 2 Samuel 7:1-14a (David's covenant promise), Ephesians 2:11-22 (breaking down barriers between Jews and Gentiles), Mark 6:30-34 and 53-56 (Jesus healing the sick after feeding the five thousand). This falls in the long summer season of Ordinary Time, a period focused on sustained Christian discipleship rather than major feast celebrations.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of its 79th General Convention held in Austin, Texas (June 28-July 7, 2018), where significant resolutions on immigration, LGBTQ+ inclusion, and economic justice had just passed. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert Wright's leadership, was navigating the convention's outcomes and their implementation at the diocesan level. No major bishop elections or immediate crises were dominating Episcopal news at this specific moment, though General Convention resolutions were still being processed and discussed in parishes nationwide.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical mid-summer heat and activity, with the city hosting various community events and festivals. The week before July 22 saw ongoing discussions about homelessness and affordable housing in Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood, where Saint John's Cathedral is located. Colorado's political landscape was focused on the November 2018 midterm elections, with gubernatorial and congressional races intensifying; Democrat Jared Polis was running for governor against Republican Walker Stapleton.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration's family separation policy at the southern border was dominating national headlines, with significant outcry over detained migrant children even as the policy was officially ending on June 20; images and reports continued reverberating through July. The investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election was ongoing, with Special Counsel Robert Mueller's team actively investigating and the Trump administration repeatedly attacking the investigation's legitimacy. Major League Baseball's All-Star Game was held on July 17, 2018 in Washington, D.C., and cultural attention was turning toward midterm election season (roughly 100 days away).

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The FIFA World Cup had concluded just two weeks prior (July 15) with France defeating Croatia; the tournament had dominated global attention through early July. A youth soccer team in Thailand (the "Wild Boars") had just been rescued from a flooded cave system on July 10-13, 2018, a story that generated worldwide relief and celebration. Trade tensions between the United States and China were escalating, with tariff threats and retaliatory measures being announced throughout July 2018.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in mid-July 2018 carried a divided, contentious energy—the aftermath of family separation policies mixed with celebration of the World Cup and the Thai soccer rescue, reflecting a nation oscillating between crisis and normalcy. Denver's progressive community and Episcopal parishes were particularly attuned to immigration justice issues, making the border crisis a pastoral concern for many congregants. The zeitgeist was one of high political polarization and anxiety about the November midterms, with summer providing only temporary respite from the intensity that had characterized the first 18 months of the Trump presidency.
